metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the compute environment for the new trusted server and add an explicit `openssh-client` (≥ 8.9) install step to its Dockerfile/runtime spec, since the analogous review_bot Dockerfile does not include it and `sshsig.sign` requires `ssh-keygen` on PATH.
findings.finding: The signing mechanism requires `ssh-keygen ≥ 8.9` to be present in the server's runtime environment (sshsig.py:60-71 enforces this via `ensure_available()`), but the plan names no compute environment (Lambda, ECS, EC2 container) for the new trusted server and provides no specification that the deployment image/environment installs OpenSSH. The analogous review_bot Dockerfile (`infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot`) — which the plan explicitly mirrors — uses `python:3.12-slim` and installs only `git curl ca-certificates`, not `openssh-client`. Without an explicit provision step, the signing call would fail closed with `SshKeygenUnavailable` at every request in production.
findings.location: Context section (AWS infra decisions) / AC3 (signing path) / infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot line 35
findings.suggested_fix: Add `openssh-client` to the trusted server's Dockerfile (e.g. `apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends git curl ca-certificates openssh-client`) and add an AC or IaC note that the deployment image must include OpenSSH ≥ 8.9. Also add a startup `sshsig.ensure_available()` call so the server refuses to boot rather than failing per-request.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the HTTP handler's request/response schema (e.g., as a Pydantic model or JSON schema snippet), explicitly listing which fields the client supplies (routing inputs like `ticket_id`) and which the server always re-derives from authoritative sources (e.g., `material_fingerprint`, `merged_log_commit`), co-locating this with the AC2 integrity decision rule.
findings.finding: The HTTP handler's request/response schema is never defined, so the plan's core decision rule ('ignore client-provided state, recompute from authoritative sources') cannot be correctly implemented or verified — an implementer cannot determine which fields are 'client inputs to be ignored' vs. required routing parameters.
findings.location: AC2 — integrity test / Transport section (library-mode HTTP handler)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Handler schema' subsection to the plan specifying the request body (e.g., `{ticket_id: str, manifest: list[str]}` as the only client inputs) and explicitly noting that `material_fingerprint` and `merged_log_commit` are always server-derived and never read from the request.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the trusted server's execution environment (container/Lambda/EC2) and explicitly require `openssh-client` (OpenSSH >= 8.9) in the image/runtime — either add it to the Dockerfile (`apt-get install -y openssh-client`) or note the AMI/base image that provides it, to satisfy `sshsig.ensure_available()`'s runtime precondition.
findings.finding: The plan asserts ssh-keygen -Y sign will be invokable in the trusted server's AWS runtime, but does not specify the execution environment (Lambda, ECS/Fargate, EC2) or require openssh-client in the container image. The reference pattern (Dockerfile.reviewbot) installs only git/curl/ca-certificates — openssh-client is absent, so ssh-keygen would be missing at runtime and sshsig.ensure_available() would raise SshKeygenUnavailable.
findings.location: Context / AWS infra decisions section; AC3 (signing path); infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot
findings.suggested_fix: Add `openssh-client` to the trusted server's Dockerfile (or equivalent): `apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends openssh-client` and add a build-time smoke test `RUN ssh-keygen -Y sign --help 2>&1 | grep -q sign`. Also add a startup assertion calling `sshsig.ensure_available()` in the HTTP handler's lifespan so a misconfigured deployment fails fast rather than at first signing request.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC2 whether the integrity test uses a live authoritative remote or a stub; if a stub, add a separate integration/smoke test (or document in AC6's operator-attested evidence) that the handler performs a real remote fetch against an actual repo and ticket system.
findings.finding: AC2's integrity test does not specify whether the authoritative remote fetch is live or mocked. If the fetch is satisfied by a test double (stub returning canned ticket data), the test validates the recomputation and signing logic but never exercises the actual remote-fetch code path — the core trust guarantee of this service. The plan provides no rationale explaining why a stub is faithful to production for this boundary.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2

findings.finding: AC1's test ('a test asserts a client-supplied doctored input does NOT change the signed verdict') can be satisfied by an offline unit test that stubs/mocks the authoritative remote fetch, never exercising the actual network boundary to ticket state or repo. This is the proxy-validation anti-pattern: the live path — 'fetch ticket state + code-at-commit from authoritative remotes itself' — is the core new behavior, and offline-green coverage that never connects to a real remote lets the work close green while the live fetch path is broken.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1 to read: '…a test (with a live or realistic-remote fixture) confirms the server fetches ticket state and code-at-commit itself and a client-supplied doctored payload does NOT change the signed verdict; the live-fetch path is exercised end-to-end, not only through mocked stubs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm why AWS Secrets Manager is needed over the existing SSM SecureString pattern (infra/terraform/ssm.tf + /rebar/prod/* IAM grant already covers all other secrets); if no strong reason exists, store the Ed25519 key as a SecureString under /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key to reuse established IAM, Terraform patterns, and operator tooling.
findings.finding: The plan introduces AWS Secrets Manager as a second secret-storage service alongside the existing AWS SSM SecureString infrastructure. All 15 current production secrets live in SSM SecureString (infra/terraform/ssm.tf), the IAM grants are already scoped to SSM (`rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read` on `/rebar/prod/*`), and boto3's `ssm.get_parameter` is the established retrieval pattern. Secrets Manager is a distinct AWS service requiring a separate IAM action (`secretsmanager:GetSecretValue`), a new KMS CMK (vs the AWS-managed SSM key), different SDK calls, and separate operator runbooks — none of which exist in the codebase today. The plan gives no justification for why SSM SecureString cannot hold the Ed25519 key, which would reuse existing IAM grants, Terraform patterns, and operator tooling.
findings.location: AWS infra decisions section + AC bullet (c): 'the Ed25519 signing key in AWS Secrets Manager (KMS-CMK-encrypted at rest)'
findings.suggested_fix: Declare the Ed25519 signing key as `aws_ssm_parameter` SecureString under `/rebar/prod/opcert-signing-key` (matching the existing `local.rebar_secret_params` pattern in ssm.tf) and grant `ssm:GetParameter` + `kms:Decrypt via ssm kms:ViaService` on the server's execution role — exactly the grant already present in iam.tf. This eliminates the new KMS CMK, the Secrets Manager resource, and the new `secretsmanager:GetSecretValue` IAM action, while keeping the materialization-to-temp-file pattern unchanged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the choice of AWS Secrets Manager over the established SSM Parameter Store SecureString pattern (used for all 14 existing production secrets in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`): document what SSM SecureString cannot provide for the Ed25519 signing key, or switch to SSM SecureString with a new `/rebar/prod/opcert-signing-key` slot and the existing least-privilege `ssm:GetParameter` + `kms:Decrypt` IAM pattern.
findings.finding: The plan chooses AWS Secrets Manager for the Ed25519 signing key without considering or justifying the deviation from the established pattern. Every other production secret in this codebase uses AWS SSM Parameter Store SecureString (confirmed: ssm.tf declares all 14 production secrets as aws_ssm_parameter SecureString resources with KMS-at-rest encryption and lifecycle ignore_changes). AWS SSM SecureString already provides KMS-encrypted-at-rest storage, fine-grained IAM read policies, and the proven least-privilege pattern the plan also requires — Secrets Manager is a distinct AWS service with a different API and a different pricing model. The plan introduces Secrets Manager as a greenfield dependency with zero existing usage in infra/terraform/ without explaining why SSM SecureString is insufficient for this use case.
findings.location: Context section — 'Signing key storage + materialization. The Ed25519 private key material lives in AWS Secrets Manager, encrypted at rest by a KMS CMK.' / infra/terraform/ssm.tf
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence rationale in the Context section, e.g. 'Secrets Manager is chosen over SSM SecureString because [specific reason, e.g. built-in rotation API / resource-level policies / cross-account access].' If no such reason exists, replace with an SSM SecureString slot following the pattern in ssm.tf and extend the existing aws_iam_role_policy.ssm_params_read document.

findings.finding: The plan stores the Ed25519 signing key in AWS Secrets Manager (a new AWS service not present anywhere in the existing Terraform) while the established pattern for all other secrets — including another Ed25519 key (`/rebar/prod/gerrit-ssh-host-ed25519-key`) — is SSM SecureString with KMS-CMK encryption (`infra/terraform/ssm.tf`, `infra/terraform/iam.tf`). Introducing a second secrets-storage service for a single key that the existing SSM SecureString pattern already covers at the same security profile (KMS-encrypted at rest, IAM-scoped read, no plaintext in Terraform state) is a config-surface proliferation with no stated functional justification.
findings.location: Context section / AC item 3 ('the Ed25519 signing key material stored in AWS Secrets Manager (KMS-CMK-encrypted at rest)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add the signing key as a new entry in `local.rebar_secret_params` in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` (e.g. `/rebar/prod/opcert-signing-ed25519-key`) using the existing `aws_ssm_parameter` resource with `type = "SecureString"`. Scope the new least-privilege IAM policy to `ssm:GetParameter` on that one path + `kms:Decrypt` via `kms:ViaService`, mirroring `data.aws_iam_policy_document.ssm_params_read` already in `iam.tf`. No new AWS service required.

findings.finding: The plan specifies AWS Secrets Manager for the Ed25519 signing key, but the existing repo infra exclusively uses SSM Parameter Store SecureStrings (ssm.tf lists all secrets as `aws_ssm_parameter` resources). Secrets Manager is a new, unused service surface with different IAM actions (`secretsmanager:GetSecretValue` vs `ssm:GetParameter`), a different KMS-CMK integration path, and a different rotation API. No `aws_secretsmanager_secret` IaC stub exists. The plan does not acknowledge this divergence from the established pattern or justify the new service.
findings.location: AC line 3(c) — 'Ed25519 signing key material stored in AWS Secrets Manager (KMS-CMK-encrypted at rest)'

findings.finding: The plan does not close the network-reachability of the underlying compute service. The plan says the HTTP service is 'fronted by AWS API Gateway' but does not state whether the compute listener (Lambda function URL, ALB, or direct HTTP port) is inaccessible except via API Gateway. If the compute endpoint is independently reachable (e.g., a Lambda function URL, an ALB with open SG, or a public EC2 port), its human/admin auth is unspecified — the API Gateway SigV4 restriction only guards the Gateway's own endpoint.
findings.location: AC bullet 2 ('library-mode HTTP service … fronted by AWS API Gateway'); AWS infra decisions section
findings.suggested_fix: For Lambda: 'No function URL is provisioned; the Lambda resource policy grants lambda:InvokeFunction only to the API Gateway execution role ARN.' For EC2/ECS: 'Security group allows inbound only from the API Gateway managed prefix list or VPC endpoint; no public port is open.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the server execution environment (Lambda with a layer, or ECS/Fargate with a custom image) and add a pre-deploy probe (e.g., a Dockerfile RUN assertion or a Lambda layer smoke-test) confirming `ssh-keygen -Y sign` is invocable before the signing path is considered complete.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `ssh-keygen -Y sign` will be invocable inside the server's AWS runtime environment (Lambda or container), but does not specify the execution environment or include any probe confirming `ssh-keygen` is present there. Standard Lambda runtimes do not ship `ssh-keygen`; an ECS/Fargate container image would need to include it explicitly. This is an unvalidated, high-stakes assumption: if the binary is absent, every signing call silently fails with a subprocess error at runtime.
findings.location: Context section — 'the shipped `sshsig.sign(pae, key_path, namespace)` invokes `ssh-keygen -Y sign -f <key_path>`' and AC: signing path AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to the IaC/deployment spec naming the base image (e.g., `python:3.12-slim` + `apt-get install -y openssh-client`) or Lambda layer that provides `ssh-keygen`, and add a startup health-check that runs `ssh-keygen -Y sign --help` to fail fast if the binary is missing.

findings.finding: The plan's least-privilege IAM specification leaves the KMS key grant and the 'repo read' resource scope unspecified. The execution role needs kms:Decrypt on the CMK (to read the Secrets Manager secret) and a git/repo-read permission (to fetch code-at-commit), but neither the KMS resource ARN condition nor the repo-access mechanism (CodeCommit ARN, S3 bucket, HTTPS credential) is named.
findings.location: AC bullet 3(b): 'least-privilege IAM for the signing-secret read + repo read'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly resolve whether the trusted server's IAM principal is (a) a new Lambda execution role (exempted from the single-owner contract because it is a different service), (b) a separate EC2 instance profile, or (c) an inline policy attached to the existing `rebar-gerrit-instance-role`; update AC4 accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new server execution role (IAM role for the trusted signing server) but the existing iam.tf declares a SINGLE-OWNER EC2 instance role ('rebar-gerrit-instance-role') with a comment saying 'Downstream stories must NOT create another role — they ATTACH their own separately-named, scoped inline policies to this same role.' If the new server is a Lambda or separate EC2, this constraint is violated; if it re-uses the existing role, that is an unenumerated consumer of the existing IAM resource that requires updating.
findings.location: AC4 ('least-privilege IAM for the signing-secret read + repo read') / infra/terraform/iam.tf:1-10

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete module path for the new trusted-server HTTP handler (e.g., `src/rebar/trusted_server/app.py`) so the creation target is explicit and verifiable.
findings.finding: The new library-mode HTTP handler (the trusted server) is specified as a creation but no module path or file location is named anywhere in the plan. Without a concrete target path (e.g., src/rebar/trusted_server/app.py), implementers have no anchor and reviewers cannot verify the artifact was created in the right place.
findings.location: AC1 / Context ('library-mode HTTP service … mirroring the proven review_bot pattern')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to AC1 such as: 'The handler lives at `src/rebar/trusted_server/app.py` (mirroring `src/rebar/review_bot/app.py`).' and reference it in the IaC deployment wiring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the handler's request schema (HTTP method, endpoint path, required JSON fields and their types, e.g. `{"ticket_id": str, "commit_sha": str}`) so an executor can implement and test it without ambiguity.
findings.finding: The HTTP handler's request schema is unspecified: the plan says the client 'invokes' the handler and can send a 'doctored material_fingerprint', but never states what fields the request payload contains (e.g., ticket ID, commit SHA, material_fingerprint), their types, or whether they are required vs. optional. An executor must invent this interface.
findings.location: Context section ('library-mode HTTP service — rebar imported as a library behind a thin HTTP handler') and AC2 ('the handler is invoked with a doctored material_fingerprint')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a minimal request/response schema block to the Context section, e.g.: `POST /certify  Body: {"ticket_id": "<str>", "commit_sha": "<git-sha>"}  Response: {"op_cert": "<armored-sshsig>", "material_fingerprint": "<sha256>"}`. State that `material_fingerprint` in the body is ignored (handler recomputes it).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` to the `aws_secretsmanager_secret` resource holding the Ed25519 signing key in the IaC declaration (AC item 4), and add a note that key rotation requires an explicit lifecycle-guard removal step.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a Secrets Manager resource holding the Ed25519 signing key but never declares `prevent_destroy` on it. Destroying this resource (e.g., accidental `terraform destroy` or refactor) permanently deletes the private key; any op-certs signed with it become unverifiable and the key cannot be recovered.
findings.location: AWS infra decisions / AC item 4: 'Ed25519 signing key in AWS Secrets Manager (KMS-CMK-encrypted at rest)'
findings.suggested_fix:
In the IaC for the Secrets Manager secret, add:
```hcl
lifecycle {
  prevent_destroy = true
}
```
Document in a runbook that intentional key rotation must first remove this guard, migrate verifiers to the new public key, then apply.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly declare the health endpoint's access class in AC1 or AC4 — either 'public-with-rationale (no sensitive data returned; required for ALB health probes)' or 'authenticated via the same SigV4 authorizer (requires a separate internal health-check mechanism)'.
findings.finding: The plan does not declare the access level of the HTTP service's health/liveness probe endpoint. The review_bot pattern (which the plan explicitly mirrors) exposes an unauthenticated `/health` route. If the new trusted server follows the same pattern but the API Gateway IAM authorizer is applied globally to all routes, the health endpoint would require SigV4 — breaking standard load-balancer health probes. If the health endpoint is exempted from the IAM authorizer (the operationally necessary choice), it becomes an unauthenticated path on the network-exposed service. The plan says nothing about which of these two postures is intended.
findings.location: AC1 — 'The trusted server is a library-mode HTTP service … fronted by AWS API Gateway'; AC4 — 'endpoint authentication via AWS IAM SigV4 … restricted to a named admin role'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'The /health endpoint is public (no IAM authorizer) and returns only {status: ok}; it MUST NOT return version, key IDs, or any server-internal state. The IAM authorizer is applied exclusively to the signing route(s).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a key rotation / retirement section: specify how the Secrets Manager secret is updated (new version or new secret ARN), how verifiers obtain the replacement public key, and whether op-certs signed under old key material remain valid.
findings.finding: The signing key in AWS Secrets Manager has no specified RETIRE/rotation path. If the Ed25519 private key is compromised or must be rotated, the plan does not describe how the secret is updated, how the new public key is distributed to verifiers, or what happens to op-certs signed under the old key.
findings.location: AWS infra decisions section / AC item (d): 'Ed25519 signing key in AWS Secrets Manager'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific .tf file(s) to create/extend for the new IaC resources (e.g., `infra/terraform/trusted_server.tf` for API GW + Secrets Manager + CMK + IAM signing role), so AC4's test can assert against a concrete file.
findings.finding: The plan's AC4 says 'The AWS IaC (infra/) declares…' the new resources (API Gateway, aws_secretsmanager_secret, KMS CMK, IAM role/policy for the signing server). None of these resources exist anywhere in infra/terraform/ today (confirmed by search). The plan names 'infra/' as the location but never specifies which .tf file(s) will be created or edited, leaving the IaC edit targets unnamed.
findings.location: AC4 / 'The AWS IaC (infra/) declares…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'New resources live in `infra/terraform/trusted_server.tf`; the AC4 test greps that file for `aws_secretsmanager_secret`, `aws_kms_key`, the API GW IAM authorizer, and absence of `kms:Sign`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit error handling for each external call site: (a) Secrets Manager fetch with retry/backoff and a timeout; (b) authoritative remote fetch with a configurable timeout and graceful error response; (c) `ssh-keygen` subprocess with `timeout=` parameter and `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` handling; surface all failures as structured error responses (not bare 500s).
findings.finding: No error handling, retry/backoff, or timeout is specified for any of the three new external call sites: (1) AWS Secrets Manager secret fetch, (2) authoritative remote fetch (ticket state + code-at-commit), and (3) the `ssh-keygen` subprocess invocation. Each is a hard dependency; a transient failure or hang in any of them will propagate as an unhandled exception or indefinite block to the caller.
findings.location: Context section — materialization path; AC: 'The handler fetches ticket state + code-at-commit from authoritative remotes ITSELF'; AC: signing path

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability AC: structured logs (including request-id, outcome, latency) for each signing request, a metric/alarm for signing errors and secret-fetch failures, and at minimum one CloudWatch alarm wired to the error metric.
findings.finding: No observability (structured logging, metrics, or alerts) is specified for the new failure points: secret fetch latency/errors, authoritative remote fetch errors, signing subprocess failures, and op-cert issuance counts. Operators have no way to detect a degraded or broken signing service short of an external end-to-end test.
findings.location: Entire plan — no observability section or AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or task to capture the key architectural decisions (KMS Ed25519 limitation, temp-file materialization rationale, library-mode HTTP service choice) in an ADR or CLAUDE.md section that lives alongside the code in `infra/` or the project root.
findings.finding: The novel architectural decisions — temp-file key materialization pattern, choice of Ed25519/SSHSIG over AWS KMS managed signing, and the library-mode HTTP service pattern — are explained inline in the Context section but are not planned to be captured in a durable artifact (ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design doc). Future maintainers who encounter the IaC or the signing code without reading this ticket will lack the rationale for why KMS was rejected and why the temp-file pattern was chosen.
findings.location: Context section — AWS infra decisions; no ADR/doc update AC present
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0001-opcert-signing-sshsig-not-kms.md` capturing: (1) KMS doesn't support Ed25519 SSHSIG, (2) temp-file materialization lifecycle, (3) library-mode HTTP rationale. Reference it from `infra/README.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address crash-safety for temp key file: either use an in-memory tmpfs mount (e.g. /dev/shm or a RAM-backed tmpfs), add a server startup sweep that deletes stale signing temp files, or document that EBS/instance storage is encrypted and ephemeral storage is wiped on termination as a compensating control.
findings.finding: The 'delete in a finally' cleanup for the materialized temp key file is not crash-safe: a SIGKILL, OOM kill, or host restart between materialization and the finally block leaves Ed25519 private key material on disk indefinitely. The plan and its test ('a test asserts no key file remains after signing') only cover the normal-exit and exception paths, not ungraceful process death.
findings.location: AWS infra decisions — signing key storage + materialization; AC item: 'deletes it in a finally; a test asserts no key file remains after signing'
findings.suggested_fix: Mount a small tmpfs volume for the temp file path; set the mkstemp directory to that mount so the file lives only in RAM and is automatically cleared on process or host termination.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify non-happy-path HTTP responses for the admin endpoint: define the error shape and HTTP status codes for at least (a) Secrets Manager unavailable, (b) authoritative remote fetch failure/timeout, and (c) signing failure; add at least one handler test for a dependency-failure scenario.
findings.finding: The HTTP handler's non-happy-path error states are unspecified for the admin user. The plan describes the success path (fetch → verify → sign → return op-cert) and one integrity test (doctored fingerprint), but does not specify what response the handler returns when: (a) Secrets Manager is unreachable/throttled, (b) the authoritative remote fetch fails or times out, (c) the signing step fails, or (d) the IAM SigV4 authentication is rejected at API Gateway. An admin invoking the endpoint receives no specified feedback in these cases.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC item (handler fetches ticket state); AC item (operator-attested round-trip)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC items specifying failure-path tests for the new HTTP handler: (a) remote fetch failure returns a well-defined error and no op-cert is issued; (b) Secrets Manager fetch failure is handled safely (no partial key material left on disk); (c) malformed/missing request parameters return a structured error response.
findings.finding: The new HTTP handler is a user-facing flow whose specified tests cover only one success-variant scenario (doctored fingerprint, infrastructure fully operational). No failure-path tests are specified for: (a) authoritative remote fetch failure (network error, unknown commit, auth failure); (b) Secrets Manager secret fetch failure; (c) malformed or missing request parameters; (d) SigV4 auth rejection. This leaves the handler's error contract entirely untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (handler integrity test) and AC1 (library-mode HTTP service)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2 (or a new AC): 'A test stubs the authoritative-remote fetch to throw a network error and asserts the handler returns HTTP 502/503 with no op-cert body and no temp key file on disk. A separate test stubs Secrets Manager to throw and asserts HTTP 500 with no temp key file remaining. A test sends a request missing the commit ref and asserts HTTP 400.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement to the IaC declaration (AC item 4): API Gateway access logging to CloudWatch Logs, a 5xx error-rate alarm, and (optionally) a latency alarm wired to the existing SNS alerts topic.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability (API Gateway access logs, CloudWatch metrics, or alarms) for the new trusted HTTP signing service. Failures, latency spikes, or unauthorized-access attempts would be invisible.
findings.location: AC item 1: 'The trusted server is a library-mode HTTP service fronted by AWS API Gateway'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the IaC plan whether the new signing service resources are co-located in the existing `rebar/prod` Terraform state or isolated in a separate state key/workspace/module, and justify the choice with respect to blast-radius.
findings.finding: The plan does not address environment (dev/staging/prod) isolation or a separate Terraform state key for the new signing service. The existing infra uses a single `rebar/prod/terraform.tfstate` key; co-locating the new API Gateway, execution role, and Secrets Manager resource in that same state couples a PoC-grade Gerrit EC2 instance with a high-assurance signing service in one blast radius.
findings.location: Context section / AC item 4: 'The AWS IaC (infra/) declares…'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add API Gateway throttling limits (default method-level throttle or usage plan) to the IaC declaration (AC item 4) to bound per-second invocations and protect downstream Secrets Manager rate limits.
findings.finding: The plan proposes an API Gateway endpoint but does not address throttling, usage plans, or invocation cost bounds for the signing service. An unbounded public endpoint (even SigV4-authenticated) can accumulate unexpected AWS costs or saturate downstream resources (Secrets Manager GetSecretValue, ssh-keygen processes) under load.
findings.location: AC item 1: 'fronted by AWS API Gateway'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit requirement that the signing handler suppresses boto3/botocore response logging at or above the secret fetch call (e.g., set `logging.getLogger('botocore').setLevel(logging.WARNING)` before the call and restore after, or use a context manager) AND that exception handlers on this path scrub or avoid logging key material; assert in the no-key-file-remains test that no key content appears in captured log output.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify log-suppression requirements on the server-side key-fetch path: boto3/botocore at DEBUG level will echo the Secrets Manager GetSecretValue response body (containing the raw Ed25519 private key) to stdout/stderr; an unhandled exception in the signing handler could dump locals (key bytes, temp path) into CloudWatch log streams. The plan says 'deletes it in a finally' but says nothing about ensuring the key never appears in structured or unstructured logs.
findings.location: Plan context section — 'at signing time the server fetches the secret and materializes it to a 0600-mode temp file'; AC3 signing path spec
findings.suggested_fix: In the handler spec, add: 'The boto3 call that fetches the signing secret MUST suppress botocore response logging for the duration of the call (set botocore logger to WARNING or higher, restore after). Exception handlers on the signing path MUST NOT log the raw secret bytes or the temp file path after materialization. The test for AC3 SHOULD capture log output and assert no substring of the Ed25519 key material appears.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify fallback behavior for each signing sub-step failure (Secrets Manager fetch error, temp-file creation failure, `SshKeygenUnavailable`) — at minimum, the HTTP status code returned and whether the temp file is guaranteed deleted — and add a test asserting the key file is cleaned up even when `sshsig.sign` raises.
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for failed sub-steps in the signing path: Secrets Manager fetch failure, temp-file materialization failure (e.g., full `/tmp`), or `ssh-keygen` unavailability (`SshKeygenUnavailable` raised by `ensure_available()`) — leaving the handler's error behavior undefined.
findings.location: Context section — 'Signing key storage + materialization' / AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each of ACs 2–5, add an explicit proving command (e.g. `pytest tests/trusted_server/test_integrity.py -v`, `pytest tests/trusted_server/test_key_cleanup.py -v`, `pytest tests/infra/test_iac_declarations.py -v`, `pytest tests/test_local_path.py -v`) so done-ness is verifiable by running a named command.
findings.finding: ACs 2–5 each promise 'a test asserts…' but none provides an explicit test file path or run command (e.g., `pytest tests/trusted_server/test_integrity.py -v`). Without a concrete proving command, a reviewer cannot verify done-ness in-session by running a specific command, and an executor cannot confirm which test file satisfies each AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 ('a test asserts the signed op-cert's bound material_fingerprint equals the authoritative one'), AC3 ('a test asserts no key file remains after signing'), AC4 ('A test asserts the IaC files declare these'), AC5 ('default local path stays offline … (test)')
findings.suggested_fix: Append a `proving_command:` line to each AC. For AC4, also anchor the IaC grep pattern, e.g.: `grep -E '"secretsmanager"' infra/*.tf && ! grep -E '"kms:Sign"' infra/*.tf` to avoid substring false-positives.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observable check to AC1 (e.g., 'a test or code assertion confirms the handler invokes rebar functions directly as a library import and does NOT exec/subprocess the stdio rebar-mcp binary') so the architectural constraint is independently verifiable.
findings.finding: AC1 is framed as an architectural implementation description ('The trusted server IS a library-mode HTTP service… fronted by AWS API Gateway') rather than as a specific observable outcome with a proving check. It names no test or command that would confirm this property holds — making it harder to evaluate done-ness independently of ACs 2–6.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'A test (e.g., `test_no_subprocess_invocation.py`) patches `subprocess.run`/`subprocess.Popen` and asserts it is never called during a certify request, confirming library-mode invocation.'

findings.finding: The existing `sshsig.sign(pae, key_path, namespace)` API (in `src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py`) takes a **filesystem path** to the Ed25519 private key. The plan requires the server to read key material from AWS Secrets Manager at signing time. The integration step — fetching the secret, writing it to a temporary file (or adapting the API to accept in-memory key bytes), invoking `ssh-keygen -Y sign`, and securely erasing the temp file — is a concrete edit target not named anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Context section ('server reads it at signing time and runs SSHSIG') / src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py / src/rebar/attest/opcert.py line 123

findings.finding: The plan states the server 'reads [the Ed25519 key] at signing time and runs SSHSIG,' but the existing sshsig.sign() implementation (attest/sshsig.py:80-86) invokes `ssh-keygen -Y sign -f <key_path>` — it requires a FILESYSTEM PATH, not in-memory bytes. The plan is silent on how Secrets Manager raw key bytes are bridged to a key_path that ssh-keygen can consume. This bridging step (e.g., write to a 0600 ephemeral temp file, sign, securely delete; or load into an ssh-agent and use SSH_AUTH_SOCK) is security-sensitive (key exposure window, crash-leaving-key-on-disk) and load-bearing for mechanism correctness. Without specifying it, an implementer may make an unsafe or incorrect choice.
findings.location: Context section — 'Signing key storage' bullet; AC3 ('server performing SSHSIG signing itself')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-bullet under 'Signing key storage': 'At signing time, the server materialises the secret bytes into a 0600 temporary file under a tmpfs-backed path (e.g., /dev/shm or os.O_TMPFILE on Linux), passes its path to sign_opcert(), and deletes the file in a finally block — so the unencrypted key is never written to the EBS data volume and survives only for the duration of the signing call.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that the server 'reads [the Ed25519 private key material from AWS Secrets Manager] at signing time and runs SSHSIG,' but the existing `sshsig.sign()` entry point requires a *file-path* argument (`key_path: str`) — it cannot accept in-memory bytes. The bridge from Secrets Manager API response (bytes/string) to an on-disk key file that `ssh-keygen -Y sign -f <key_path>` can consume is a novel, security-critical step (secure tempfile write, 0600 permissions, guaranteed cleanup on exceptions/signals) that is unspecified and unverified anywhere in the codebase or ADRs.
findings.location: Context section: 'Signing key storage' bullet + AC line 3(c): 'the server performing SSHSIG signing itself'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan note or ADR stub: the server writes the Secrets Manager secret value to a `tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode='wb', suffix='.pem', delete=False)` under a `tmpfs`-backed directory (e.g. `/dev/shm` on Linux), sets `chmod 0600`, calls `sshsig.sign(..., key_path=tmp.name, ...)`, and removes the file in a `finally` block — or uses a `with` context that guarantees deletion. Confirm the secret's stored format matches OpenSSH private key format (the `ssh-keygen` expectation).

findings.finding: The plan does not declare how Ed25519 private key material is handled on disk between its retrieval from AWS Secrets Manager and consumption by `ssh-keygen -Y sign -f <key_path>`. Because `sshsig.sign()` requires a filesystem path (not in-memory bytes), the server must write the raw key to disk; without specifying tmpfs/ramfs, O_TMPFILE, or an explicit secure-deletion step, the private key can persist on the EC2 instance's root volume after signing.
findings.location: Context section ('the server reads it at signing time and runs SSHSIG') + AC3 ('Ed25519 signing key material stored in AWS Secrets Manager … with the server performing SSHSIG signing itself') + src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:80-86
findings.suggested_fix: In the signing server's key-retrieval + sign flow: write the key bytes to a `tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(dir='/dev/shm', delete=False)` (Linux shared-memory FS, never swapped to disk), call `sshsig.sign(pae, tmp.name, namespace)`, then `os.unlink(tmp.name)` in a `finally` block. Document this in the IaC runbook. Alternatively, use a dedicated tmpfs mount for the signing temp dir.

findings.finding: AC1's test decision-rule is underspecified: 'a test asserts a client-supplied doctored input does NOT change the signed verdict' names neither (i) what constitutes 'doctored input' (fake ticket state? substitute commit SHA? both?), (ii) which invariant is asserted (byte-identical signature? same verdict string? manifest unchanged?), nor (iii) which signing path the assertion targets. The codebase has two distinct signing paths — HMAC-SHA256 via `signing.sign_manifest` and SSHSIG-opcert via `signing.sign_opcert_manifest` — and the plan does not say which one the HTTP handler uses, so the test oracle is structurally ambiguous.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'The test sends a request with a body containing a fabricated ticket-state blob and/or a substitute commit SHA; it asserts that the resulting DSSE envelope's in-toto subject digest (opcert_subject_digest) reflects the server-fetched state, not the client-supplied value — verified by calling sign_opcert_manifest (the SSHSIG path) and confirming the returned envelope does not match one constructed from the doctored inputs.'

findings.finding: The SSHSIG signing path (`ssh-keygen -Y sign -f <key_path>`) requires the Ed25519 private key as a file on disk, but the plan stores it in AWS Secrets Manager. The plan does not describe the bridge: read key material from Secrets Manager → write to a secure temp file (0600) → pass path to ssh-keygen → zero and unlink the file. `ssh-keygen -Y sign` has no stdin-key or env-var-key mode; `-f` is mandatory and must be a filesystem path. Without this materialization step the signing call will fail at runtime.
findings.location: Context section (AWS infra decisions) + AC line 3 — 'the server performing SSHSIG signing itself'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan section describing the signing-time key-materialization flow: fetch PEM from Secrets Manager → `tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode='wb', suffix='.pem', delete=False)` with `os.chmod(path, 0o600)` → call `sign_opcert(..., key_path=tmp.name)` → `os.unlink(tmp.name)` in a finally block. Note this in the IaC section so the server's IAM execution role includes `secretsmanager:GetSecretValue` for the key secret.

findings.finding: No proving command is specified for any acceptance criterion. AC1 references 'a test' and AC4 references '(test/asserted)' but neither names a file path, test ID, or runnable command. AC2 and AC3 name no verification step at all. A reviewer cannot confirm done-ness without re-deriving the checks from scratch.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all four ACs

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
